title: So You Want to Take Iran's Oil… || Peter Zeihan

transcript.text: Hey all, Peter Zion here coming to you
from Colorado. Um, Donald Trump is going
on about taking other count's oil again,
specifically Iran. Uh, and
there's no good way to do it. Let's just
start with that. But it is important, I
think, to understand where the stuff is
and what that would mean for a potential
military occupation. So, there are
basically two large concentrations of
petroleum in Iran. The first is kind of
in the middle part of the Persian Gulf,
directly opposite the country of gutter.
That's one look kind of like a thumb
sticking up on the south side of the
Gulf. This is what the Iran call the
South Parsfield and the surrounding
fields. It's majority offshore. It is
operated by a lot of foreign companies
because the Iranians don't know how to
do it themselves. But this is
responsible for somewhere between 70 and
80% of the country's natural gas
production. Now, Iran doesn't really
export natural gas in the conventional
sense. And almost everything that come
from South Pars is fed into the local
pipeline network in order to be burned
to generate electricity. So if the
United States wanted to take over this
zone, it would basically be shutting off
the natural gas production because
there's no export capacity. Uh the
nearest country would be Turkey. There
is a little pipeline there, but you'd
have to go through a lot of Iran to get
to it. Uh and second, there's no
liqufied natural gas facility like
exists on the south side of the Gulf.
So, if you take this thing, uh, you're
just shutting it down and triggering,
um, war crimes level of humanitarian
disaster as you turn off the power in a
country with roughly 90 million people.
That'd be bad. Uh, the other one is
easier in simply because it's, you know,
possible. Again, not an endorsement
here. Uh, it's in the province of
Kustan, which is in the country's
southwest, hard up against the Iraq
border, directly opposite from Bastra,
if you remember your war in Iraq days.
uh Kustan has 70 to 80% of the country's
oil production generates a little bit of
waste natural gas here and there but
it's mostly about the oil and this is
the stuff that basically powers the
Iranian economy oil from Kustan is
consumed locally it's consumed
throughout the rest of the country it is
sent to refineries the country over and
a lot of it is exported through Carg
Island
is an island off the coast of the
northern Gulf It's Iranian. Uh, and
people have been talking about that a
lot recently. Uh, Donald Trump even
knows where it is and he seems to think
that if you take car, you control the
oil industry. No, no, no. If you take
car, you can shut off Iran's ability to
export, but that doesn't give you
control over production. So, if your
goal is to take the oil, you have to
basically capture all of Kustan province
and a little chunks of territory that
are adjacent to it. Now, Kazakhstan is
interesting for a number of reasons
besides the oil. If you remember back to
your uh political geography days, Iran
is a series of mountain nations,
different ethnicities that bit by bit
were amalgamated into the whole that we
now call Iran or Persia if you want to
use the older term. Kustan is an outlier
there because it's flat. it's not
mountainous and the vast bulk of the
population are Arabs instead of mountain
peoples or Persians. So they are an
oppressed minority living in the country
and they live on top of the oil and they
get so little of the money that comes
from the oil that this is one of the few
parts of Iran that's actually
experiencing population decline because
basically the Iranian government tan
siphons off the oil, leaves nothing for
the Arabs and they're just kind of like
wallowing in their own poverty. Uh
before you think, "Oh, this is a great
fifth column to uh launch a rebellion
against Thrron," keep in mind that the
United States has tried that trick
specifically before just on the other
side of the river in southern Iraq where
you have a Shia majority that used to be
ruled by a Sunni government in Baghdad.
And after 20 years, about the only thing
that the Shia of Iraq could agree on is
that they hated the United States more
than everybody else. I can guarantee you
in the time that the United States has
been resting and recouping in the
aftermath of the war and terror, we have
not gotten any better at nation
building. And when we were trying to
occupy southern Iraq, which supposedly
had a restive political group that hated
the central government that we had
overthrown, uh it didn't go nearly as
well as we had hoped. And this time, if
you do that in Kustan, there's a lot
more Iranians with a lot more weaponry
and equipment that can be brought to
bear. Because in the case of Iraq, we'd
overthrew the entire government and were
the authority. In the case of Iran, we'd
have tens of thousands of American
troops on the ground, occupying a local
population, and then resisting the
general forces of the rest of the
country. Anyway, bottom line of all of
this is not that I think we can or
should take Iran's oil. Just to give you
an idea of what is in play. Uh it's
pretty clear that Donald Trump is
planning some sort of ground offensive.
He has never deployed troops to an area
and not use them. And in this case,
we've got two loads of Marines with uh
the Marine Expeditionary Units on their
way. One of them with the Tripoli is
practically local now. They were in
Diego Garcia last week. And the other
group, the boxer, is approaching
Southeast Asia and is expected to be in
the Persian Gulf in two to three weeks.
Uh, and of course, the uh, the airborne
forces can be wherever they need to be.
Uh, so we're definitely moving forces
in. The Trump administration is
definitely planning on using them, it
will definitely be a disaster. And if
the Trump administration decides to go
after this target specifically, we're
going to be in occupation in the Middle
East just like we were for the bulk of
the last 25 years. And we all remember
how that went.
